posts.name: Gautier Roussille
posts.cooked:
Hi Glen,
Glad you enjoyed our Charleston
About DIAM, we use it for one specific UK-based client who requires it. He order pre-bottling and is our largest client. We use DIAM5, as per our experience DIAM10+ are too tight for our wines.
I also used DIAM exclusively when I was working in the Côte de Beaune.
What I can say is that DIAM is a solid solution, however:
-Some people can always tell (blind) if a wine has been corked with DIAM (not just hearsay, I’ve seen it). So DIAM is TCA free but there are other molecules involved, maybe the glue?
-We can now obtain natural corks that have been tested for TCA/TCB with guaranteed levels. There are other forms of corkage but that leave us with a low enough percentage that I fell acceptable. The corkage feedback we have on our bottle is pretty low and eventhough this doesn’t really apply to our customers overseas, I’ve always accepted to exchange a corked bottle that have been brought back to me.
-As we’ve been using DIAM for 10+years here, we can compare on several vintages. For now, I prefer natural cork on every vintage (which wasn’t the case in Beaune so style might play a role here). I will probably take the opportunity of our upcoming celebration to check on that again.
-Of course the cost of high quality, TCA-tested cork is higher than DIAM but I’m willing to pay for a natural product.

posts.name: Gautier Roussille
posts.cooked:
So… about 2021 harvest.
As I already stated and I’m sure you’ve hear from others, 2021 was a challenging vintage, because of the frost, sure, but mainly because of the rain. The consequences in terms of maturity are twofold. First, rain means no sun and lower temperature, delaying maturity, but a lot of rain means more downy mildew (and surprisingly mildew!!*), damaging the leaves and potentially destroying the vines’ capacity to ripen grapes.

This is one of the reason we had to fight mildew so hard and so late in the season this year. For the first time we begun harvest already tired… but I’m glad we didn’t capitulate as the resulting grapes were lovely. We choose to pick late (for the first time almost as late as Thévenet as we both started when everyone else was done). I believe it was the right choice as many choose to pick early by fear of the botrytis or lack of leaves but ended up with really really low brix. So we kept an eye on the sky and postponed picking several time until we achieved satisfying maturity (everything ended up around 13,5%). We were lucky enough to have a lovely sunny week to pick that unfortunately ended with another round of heavy rain (2-3 days, I’ll need to come back to you on exact dates, I’m in the train and away from my notebook), after wich we hurried to pick the last remaining bunches (took 3days).

In the end, we got nice, ripe grapes, with plenty of noble rot on some blocks and perfectly healthy one on others (I have a pictures or two I’ll share here). Amusingly the block with the most perfect grapes ended up to be the wettest one, Champ-rond, that we use for our Retour a la Terre cuvee. Maybe it felt just like a regular year for these vines, anyway, good for us as it’s the block we always pick last (this year we had to get the casses of grapes out of the block by hand as no machine could go in this deep of mud and water !).

About the wines, they just finished fermenting (just a few grams of sugar left on Charleston) and I expect something akin to 2016 where the noble rot was also present: exotic, exuberant wines that taste great young and never really shut down. If I had the courage, I would probably sell the 2021 before the 2020 but this would take so much effort that I’ll probably forget about it. I’ll tell you more in a few weeks/month when we’ll be actually able to taste the wine and get a glimpse of how it will show.

About our 30 vintages tasting:
-I can again confirm that our wines age beautifully over 30years. One notable exception is 2000, a vintage that was hyped due to the millennium but that my parents in law never really enjoyed. It was still good five years ago but if you have some bottles, don’t wait to open them.
-The coldest vintages develop a surprising toastiness after 15+years (2004 yes, 2011 not yet). One hypothesis would be that this is an evolution of the thiols we might have on these less ripe vintages.

*Mildew is generally and issue in dry, windy conditions but one of the way we fight mildew is by spraying sulphur, which is activated by the sun…
